Regarding FOB-Gate: A Review and Response

# Regarding FOB-Gate: A Review and Response

[](https://substack.com/@frontierdharma)

![Vishal Ganesan's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wJoy!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F750f3d10-4e5d-47b9-99cf-129733e051af_400x398.jpeg)

[Vishal Ganesan](https://substack.com/@frontierdharma)

Jan 01, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

16

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

It’s been an eventful few weeks at Frontier Dharma!

The firestorm began when I tweeted the below, in response to Sahana Singh’s comments on JD Vance’s comments regarding his wife Usha’s religion:

Thanks for reading Frontier Dharma ! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Subscribe

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!g_q7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F37a83656-035a-4cc0-9875-35b7f75432b0_1170x1884.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!g_q7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F37a83656-035a-4cc0-9875-35b7f75432b0_1170x1884.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

I then followed up with another:

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TxnD!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F120401d0-e43f-43cf-97cf-7a6c4d0282b0_1170x1552.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TxnD!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F120401d0-e43f-43cf-97cf-7a6c4d0282b0_1170x1552.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In response to a critique of my position, I then tweeted the following, which is too long to include in a screenshot:

> “Hindus face a lot of challenges in America. I’ve spent years
> documenting them. But the one of the most serious and under
> appreciated challenges is precisely this mentality. See for the
> example the easy conflation of ‘civilizational heritage’ and ‘roots,’
> or the obsession with being ‘unapologetic.’ What this translates to
> for the first generation is the mindless transmission and mimicry of
> whatever traditions they grew up with in India. It reflects a
> conception of pluralism that is distinctly Indian, where religious
> communities live on social islands, each with their customs, values,
> and traditions, with all serious differences of belief conveniently
> swept under the rug of Hindu ‘diversity.’ Perhaps this sort of
> pluralism has its virtues in a society like India, but it is inimical
> to the very idea of nationhood, a fact recognized as much by India’s
> greatest nationalist thinkers, though neglected by their present-day
> followers. If your vision of ‘Hindu America’ is Hindus living in
> self-enclosed enclaves where the continuation of tradition is seen as
> an end in and of itself, then I want no part in it. What you see as
> ‘marginalization of your own roots,’ I see as a restoration of Indic
> civilization’s natural expansiveness. What you call ‘confidence,’ I
> see as a small-minded fixation on what Aurobindo and Vivekananda
> called the ‘Hinduism of the kitchen.’ In any case, even if you don’t
> care about American society at large, then you should rethink your
> approach for your kids’ sake. I’ve spent years calling out anti-Hindu
> bigotry on the right and left, but the number 1 culprit for Hinduism’s
> growing obsolescence among the 2nd generation is the 1st generation’s
> utter failure to rethink and reformulate Hindu tradition in a way that
> resonates with the 2nd gen’s worldview, which is heavily Americanized.
> I was born and raised here, I’ve seen the fruits of your approach, and
> I reject it.”

The response was, predictably, polarized. Many of my first generation followers bristled at my derisive use of “FOB” (i.e., Fresh Off the Boat) and accused me of throwing Hindu immigrants under the bus, while others—particularly fellow second generation Hindu Americans born and raised here—expressed gratitude for expressing a view that they share but had not seen vocalized.

For those interested in hearing a more in-depth discussion of my views, you can also watch my [live stream with Mukunda of Meru Media](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mqqqL29JMn8&t=3879s) and listen to my [interview with Razib Khan](https://www.razibkhan.com/p/vishal-ganesan-and-anang-mittal-american). The original tweet and the discussion that followed also inspired responses, including from [Shawn Binda of Hindu Lifestyle](https://hindulifestyle.substack.com/p/beyond-the-parking-lot-reclaiming) and the [Hindu American Foundation](https://www.hinduamerican.org/blog/hinduism-is-diverse-hindu-americans-have-common-interests). R Jaggnathan published a response in [The Print](https://theprint.in/opinion/ndian-american-hindus-adapt-in-proselytising-world/2777949/) and Swarajya Mag published a [response](https://swarajyamag.com/culture/how-should-american-hindus-think-about-hinduism) asking “How Should American Hindus Think About Hinduism?” I imagine there were others, so if you’ve seen one that I’ve missed, please let me know, but for now I want to take the opportunity to respond to Shawn’s piece, which I found particularly thoughtful.

Shawn’s piece, titled “Beyond the Parking Lot: Reclaiming Fluency to Fundamentally Adapt Hinduism,” offers a conservative alternative to the approach I advocate in my [inaugural essay](https://frontierdharma.substack.com/p/the-hindu-case-against-hinduism-a). While Shawn largely agrees with my diagnosis of second generation alienation and understands the need for adaptation, he supports a gradualist approach that integrates first and second generation perspectives in a “relay race” model, where the first generation preserves, the second generation educates, and the subsequent generations adapt.

The rationale for Shawn’s proposed model is that any successful adaptation of Hindu tradition in the diaspora requires a grounding in the tradition, which is something only the more rooted first generation can provide. So, rather than building tradition anew, Shawn argues that we should focus on building the educational infrastructure required to transmit this knowledge. Without native fluency, Shawn asks, how can we authentically reimagine the tradition?

Renewal in this scenario is grounded in tradition and relies on religious authorities who have “authentic” understanding. Shawn argues— contrary to my understanding— that reformists like Ram Mohan Roy, Sri Aurobindo, Swami Vivekananda, and Dayananda Saraswati were deeply immersed in tradition and it was this fluency that allowed them to “authoritatively reimagine” the tradition.

I’d like to offer a few directed responses:

1.  **Marginalization Does Not Equal Erasure**

Shawn argues that by arguing that the “FOB” perspective needs to be marginalized by American Hindus, I am disrespecting the contributions of the first generation. My language in the tweets quoted above was no doubt provocative, but as I made clear in my earlier essay, I do not place any blame for the current situation on the first generation. Nor does the “FOB perspective” refer to immigrants as such, but a mode of Hindu self-understanding shaped by Indian political theology and social organization, rather than by the moral, civic, and epistemic conditions of American life.

The first generation has achieved remarkable things. Many of them came here with very little, and through sheer hard work they were able to build lives for their families and establish temples in their communities. But their frameworks remained distinctly *Indian* — embedded in the social and political logic of a homeland that no longer structures our lives.

As a result, when first-generation frameworks dominate conversations about “Hindu identity,” they unwittingly imprison the second generation within categories they cannot inhabit, rendering Hindu tradition an ethnic inheritance rather than a lived spiritual possibility.

It is the first generation’s embeddedness in these inherited frameworks that prevents them from articulating a Hindu identity legible within American discursive norms (religion, identity, conscience, pluralism, etc.). Without that re-articulation, second-generation Hindus cannot meaningfully inhabit Hindu identity within the American public sphere.

2.  **Authority and Tradition**

Shawn argues that fluency in the tradition is a prerequisite to any successful adaptation. In support of this argument he cites figures like Rammohan Roy, Swami Vivekananda, Dayananda Saraswati and Sri Aurobindo as examples of thinkers who were able to “authoritatively reimagine” the tradition only after they were firmly grounded in it. But this is a misreading of both history and the nature of spiritual authority.

The reformists Shawn mentions did not simply reinterpret texts within the constraints of tradition; they fundamentally redefined how Hindu spirituality was conceptualized. Even Swami Vivekananda, who was quite insistent in his writings that he disapproved of radical reformist groups like the Brahmo Samaj, represented a radical departure from traditional Hindu praxis. In America, his Vedanta Society abandoned temple ritual and Sanskritic liturgy for a “practical Vedanta” that appealed to America’s Protestant sensibilities.

Indeed, Vivekananda’s posthumous domestication as a nationalist icon obscures just how controversial he was during his lifetime. Not only did Vivekananda flout orthodox taboos on crossing the “black waters” to travel to America, he also dined on meat with foreigners and insisted on teaching Vedanta to people of all races and classes, all of which drew the ire of orthodox groups in India.

It was precisely these attacks that led Vivekananda, in a reply to an address of welcome at Shivaganga and Manamadura, to attack orthodox Hindus as “don’t-touchists” whose religion “is in the kitchen” and whose God “is the cooking-pot.” Indeed, Vivekananda’s emphasis on “practical Vedanta” not only drew rebuke from orthodox Advaitins (i.e., non-dualists), but from fellow monks of his order, who saw Vivekananda’s emphasis on service and action in the material world as inconsistent with their guru Ramakrishna’s message of renunciation.

The reformists, including Vivekananda, sought to reconceptualize Hindu tradition in light of the massive social and cultural shifts of the time. They were not writing esoteric commentaries for learned monks and priests, but instead sought to reach the newly dislocated class of Indians negotiating tradition in the context of colonial modernity. Rather than existing in a continuum with tradition, their project is better understood as an act of re-founding.

The authority of these figures arose not from pedigree or fluency in tradition but from their ability to translate inherited traditions into the idioms of a changing world. That is precisely our position today, but by tying authority to tradition, Shawn ignores how radically different the second generation’s frame of reference is and ends up recreating our current dilemma.

3.  **The Epistemic Break and the Crisis of Intelligibility**

Shawn’s relay race model in which successive generations work in concert to preserve and transmit religion ignores the fact that the epistemic break between the first and second generations has already occurred.

I admire and respect Shawn’s work to educate the next generation, but by his own admission he is the exception to the rule. Most second generation Hindus in the diaspora are already alienated from Hindu tradition, so why would we expect them to take on the work of not just gaining fluency in the tradition, but dedicating their lives to educating the next?

The key feature of the current crisis is a lack of intelligibility. As I wrote in my [earlier essay](https://frontierdharma.substack.com/p/the-hindu-case-against-hinduism-a), the Hindu temple is “enmeshed in a symbolic language of ritual, iconography, and even geography” and “was not built primarily for the transmission of religious doctrine across national and cultural boundaries, but rather as an act of reverential imitation.”

These traditions are preserved by the first generation as a microcosm of their native culture, but for the second generation they exist as symbols deprived of vital meaning. It is precisely this hermeneutic impasse that has given rise to secularized Hindu culture in the diaspora in the form of “South Asianism” that wholly subsumes tradition to the progressive ideological consensus.

What is the solution to this crisis of intelligibility? Shawn argues that we can bridge this gap through education, but not only do I think that’s impossible, I also think it mistakes the relationship between tradition and *understanding*.

While all traditions have at their core some immutable truth, the form these traditions take are contingent on particular historical and cultural developments. Take, for example, the practice of *murti puja*, popularly referred to as “idol worship.” For many Hindus today, it is difficult to conceive of their religious practice without *murti puja*, and yet the Vedas— the earliest Hindu texts— don’t contain any references to the practice.

The scholar Richard Davis in his book *Religions of Early India: A Cultural History* notes that while the worship of statues of folk deities like Yakshas and Yakshis was widespread, the Jains and Buddhists were in fact the first pan-India religious communities to create anthropomorphic images for ritual worship (Davis 341).

The manufacture of these images was centered in Mathura in the early centuries CE and was part of a broader visual turn in Indian spiritual traditions. Following the Jains and Buddhists, devotional communities we now recognize as Hindu began producing images of their favored deities to facilitate the personal devotional connection of their adherents.

In the Hindu case specifically, the shift in religious culture was not universally accepted among Brahmins who were embedded in the Vedic tradition. While some orders of Brahmins incorporated the worship of images into their own ritual practice and published prescriptive guides on the proper rituals for public shrines, there was a conservative backlash from other Brahminical orders who insisted that anionic Vedic sacrificial rituals were superior, and encouraged disassociation from Brahmins who participated in image worship (364).

The point here is not to argue whether *murti puja* is good or bad, but to illustrate how traditions morph over time in response to cultural shifts. The traditions we have inherited are not normative practices that are essentially constitutive of “Hinduism,” but ever-changing practices that can’t be understood in isolation from their societal context. The Brahmin orders who formulated extensive ritual guides on how to properly worship temple *murtis* were not constructing a new religious practice from above, but were themselves adapting to a broader turn in the religious culture where anthropomorphic devotional icons were increasingly ubiquitous.

In understanding tradition, then, we must first acknowledge that whatever is merely reproduced today “is never meaningful today in the same way as it was when first produced” (Mehta 146). In the diaspora in particular our social and cultural conditions are fundamentally different from that of the generations that preceded us, and we have no choice but to interpret tradition from our own perspective. Implicit in this act of understanding is an “element of creative productivity” that reinvigorates the tradition in the current context.

The focus on “understanding” also raises another point, which is that understanding is only possible when one is already alienated from the tradition. When one lives within a tradition and is immersed in its symbolic language, there is no question of understanding why one engages in a particular ritual or practice. Traditions in this context are not objects of study, but that which shapes the world around us. To ask someone immersed in a tradition to explicate it is akin to asking a fish “how’s the water?”

It is tempting to overcome this alienation through intensive cultural education. This is in fact the default approach of the diaspora today. First generation immigrants eagerly enroll their children in Sunday school, have them memorize the *Bhagavad Gita*, and bring them to temple functions in the hopes of inculcating interest in the tradition. But, as we see with the second generation today, these methods have failed comprehensively.

Rather than subjecting children to a one-way conversation where the first generation are accepted as infallible authorities on what constitutes the tradition, we must instead engage in a dialogue, where we are “not only putting questions to tradition but allowing ourselves to be questioned in turn by it” (156). This is a creative process where “new ways of speaking and a new truth emerge, not as something made by us but as that which moulds and shapes us and enables us to live by” (157).

4.  **Caribbean Hinduism as a Model of Adaptation**

One other point I wanted to address in Shawn’s piece is his use of the Sanatana Dharma Maha Sabha (SDMS) as a potential model for the North American diaspora.

Shawn’s account of the SDMS is worth quoting in full:

> The Sanatana Dharma Maha Sabha (SDMS) of Trinidad represents a
> successful institutional adaptation to diaspora conditions, achieving
> what they had set out, mainly Hindu survival and political
> representation. This strategy involved embracing institutional unity
> and also standardizing Hindu practices—some of the very things Vishal
> criticizes—to achieve political and social success.
>
> This is not meant to be a thorough exposition or analysis on this
> history, which I will save for a separate essay, but a brief synopsis
> of relevant timelines and actions that resulted in Hindus having a
> more keen sense of their identity.
>
> Prior to 1952, Hinduism in Trinidad was fragmented and localized. The
> descendants of indentured labourers brought with them local traditions
> and practices, and this presented a problem for a united Hindu voice.
> Hindu marriages were not recognized until 1945, 100 years after the
> first indentured labourers arrived on the island.
>
> Coupled with colonial pressures, there was competition from the Arya
> Samaj movement, which led to the consolidation of 2 groups (Sanatan
> Dharma Control Board and the Sanatan Dharma Association) to form the
> Sanatan Dharma Maha Sabha in 1952.

Shawn argues in the passage above that I reject institutional unity and standardization of the kind that we see in the SDMS, but nothing could be further from the truth.

We desperately need to be more unified, but all too often Hindus fail to go a level deeper and contemplate the basis for that unity. We take for granted that “Hinduism” is a religion with a unified religious community without building the practices, institutions, and philosophical foundations required to actually build the unity we seek. This is precisely why I take issue with the pervasive notion of Hindu “inclusivism,” which haphazardly includes all self-identified Hindus under the same umbrella, without considering the transformations required to achieve that unity.

It’s my view that the conceptual framework of “Hinduism” that shapes how we think about our tradition is an impediment to this work, which is why I advocate rejecting the term. In this context, the formation of the SDMS is precisely the kind of restructuring that I see as necessary, in substance if not form. So while we can and should certainly look to the SDMS as an example, we must also keep in mind that any program of reform will necessarily reflect prevailing social and political conditions.

For example, though the Hindus who were brought to the Caribbean as indentured laborers were diverse, the majority of them came from what are now Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. This facilitated the development of a creolized Hindi that would become *lingua franca* among Caribbean Hindus. The regional affinity between the immigrants also made it easier to establish a limited pantheon of major deities, with a devotional culture shaped by monastic orders that were active in North India like the Ramanandis (Van Der Veer and Vertovec 153).

Another important factor in the development of Hinduism in the Caribbean is the diminished salience of caste identity. Indentured laborers were largely brought over as individuals drawn from various regions of India. Removed from their villages and kinship networks, early immigrants coalesced, resulting in marriages and family formation across traditional caste lines (154). Unlike in India where each caste might have its own legends, histories, and myths, the emerging Hindu ethnic community in the Caribbean adopted a generalized Hindu culture.

This is no doubt a simplistic account of the development of Hinduism in the Caribbean, but the main point I want to emphasize is that any program of reform in America, for example, will necessarily be responsive to our unique conditions. Hindus in America are decidedly not an ethnic community, and rather than living in self-enclosed Hindu communities as was the case in the Caribbean, Hindus in America are by and large well assimilated into the multi-cultural American mainstream.

The appeal of gradualism of the kind Shawn advocates lies in its reassurance that nothing essential must change. But gradualism presumes continuity, and continuity is precisely what has already been lost. The task before the second generation is not to wait patiently for transmission to resume, but to reconstruct the symbolic language through which Hindu life becomes intelligible in America. This is not a rejection of inheritance, but the only way inheritance survives.

I am appreciative of Shawn’s intervention in this debate, and I believe that this is a critical conversation for us to have. I look forward to further engagement on this topic as I believe it will help us further clarify our respective positions on how best to adapt tradition to our current circumstances.

\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*

**Davis, Richard H.** *Religions of Early India: A Cultural History.* Princeton University Press, 2024.

**Mehta, J.L.** *India And the West: The Problem of Understanding.* Scholars Press, 2001.

**Van Der Veer, Peter**, and **Steven Vertovec**. “Brahmanism Abroad: On Caribbean Hinduism as an Ethnic Religion.” *Ethnology*, vol. 30, no. 2, 1991, pp. 149–166
